sourceMovie.title: Raging Bull
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Raging Bull (1980) as a drama theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The drama and history blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Cinderella Man
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Cinderella Man draws from the same drama well as Raging Bull, channeling history in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2005 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: The Smashing Machine
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Smashing Machine (2025) captures the drama appeal of Raging Bull without simply imitating it. The history streak running through both films is what puts The Smashing Machine at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: You Don't Know Jack
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama appeal of Raging Bull — that tense edge, the history — translates cleanly into You Don't Know Jack (2010). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Big George Foreman
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Big George Foreman (2023) clearly understood what makes drama work — the same quality that keeps Raging Bull rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Truman Show
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Truman Show (1998) is the kind of film Raging Bull fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The drama overlap is real, but it's the funny emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Million Dollar Baby
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Million Dollar Baby shares Raging Bull's gift for drama — the 2004 film delivers momentum without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Southpaw
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Raging Bull compelling — the drama, the action — shows up in Southpaw (2015) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that tense storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Creed II
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama energy of Raging Bull is alive in Creed II (2018), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a tense payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Rocky IV
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Rocky IV (1985) operates in the same drama as Raging Bull, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The tense payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Falling Down
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Falling Down (1993) belongs to the same conversation as Raging Bull — both anchor their drama in crime emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

faq.question: What should I watch after Raging Bull?
faq.answer: Try Cinderella Man, The Smashing Machine, You Don't Know Jack—each shares DNA with Raging Bull while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Raging Bull?
faq.answer: Raging Bull (1980) is best described as Drama, History. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Raging Bull worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.9/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
